title: The solution of the Kadison-Singer problem
speaker: Daniel Spielman
abstract: We will explain our recent solution of the Kadison-Singer Problem and the equivalent Bourgain-Tzafriri and Paving Conjectures. We will begin by introducing the method of interlacing families of polynomials and use of barrier function arguments to bound the roots of polynomials. To prove the Paving Conjecture, we introduce the Mixed Characteristic Polynomial of a collection of matrices, and use the theory of Real Stable polynomials and multivariate generalizations of the barrier function arguments to bound their roots.

proof_stages.label: Kadison-Singer and paving reductions
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces the original Kadison-Singer extension problem for pure states on the diagonal MASA in bounded operators on ell^2, then replaces it by equivalent finite-dimensional paving and vector-partition formulations.

proof_stages.label: Isotropic vector partition formulation
proof_stages.summary: The central finite-dimensional target is reformulated in terms of decompositions of the identity by small vectors and the goal of partitioning them into nearly half-isotropic parts, with large vectors and failed random partitions motivating the theorem.

proof_stages.label: Expected characteristic polynomial reduction
proof_stages.summary: A two-set partition is encoded by block random vectors, and the proof is reduced to studying the expected characteristic polynomial of a sum of independent rank-one random matrices, which depends only on the covariance matrices.

proof_stages.label: Real stability and mixed characteristic polynomials
proof_stages.summary: Using real stable polynomials, the determinant construction, stability-preserving derivative operators, and the formula for mixed characteristic polynomials, the expected characteristic polynomial is shown to be real-rooted.

proof_stages.label: Interlacing-family selection principle
proof_stages.summary: Common interlacings and the criterion that all convex combinations are real-rooted are used to organize the partition polynomials into an interlacing family, guaranteeing a leaf whose largest zero is no larger than that of the average.

proof_stages.label: Barrier bound for the largest root
proof_stages.summary: For positive semidefinite covariance matrices with sum the identity and small trace, a multivariate barrier argument tracks upper bounds under the derivative operators and proves that the largest root of the mixed characteristic polynomial is at most (1+\sqrt{\epsilon})^2.

proof_stages.label: Consequences for Weaver and paving
proof_stages.summary: Combining the interlacing selection with the root bound yields a positive-probability vector partition theorem, implying Weaver's KS2 conjecture and hence Kadison-Singer, and the same method with k choices gives Anderson paving.

examples.name: Identity matrix obstruction to paving without zero diagonal
examples.note: The identity matrix shows concretely why a diagonal/trace-zero condition is needed: every diagonal block still has norm one, so paving cannot reduce the norm.

examples.name: Large vectors as the obstruction to half-spherical partitions
examples.note: A configuration with large vectors produces badly distorted parts under any two-coloring, motivating the small-norm hypothesis in Weaver's formulation as the necessary obstruction to remove.

examples.name: Repeated elementary unit vectors defeating random partitions
examples.note: Many copies of each coordinate vector show that a uniformly random partition is likely to be bad even though a balanced partition exists, motivating a rare-event existence method rather than ordinary concentration.
examples.engagement_reason: he constructs repeated elementary basis vectors, analyzes random coloring probabilities by direction, and explains the deterministic half-and-half fix

examples.name: Expander graph edge vectors
examples.note: Vectors associated to graph edges have outer-product sum equal to the graph Laplacian, so expander edge-splitting provides an illustrative sparse case where Kadison-Singer-type partitioning was already known via the Lovasz local lemma.
examples.engagement_reason: he builds edge vectors from a graph, identifies their outer-product sum with the graph Laplacian, and relates expander eigenvalues to near isotropic position

examples.name: Bad averages of real-rooted polynomials
examples.note: Pairs of real-rooted polynomials whose averages either lose real-rootedness or fail the largest-root conclusion illustrate why common interlacing, not mere real-rootedness of individuals, is the needed condition.
examples.engagement_reason: he uses plotted real-rooted polynomial pairs to show how their averages can lose real-rootedness or have too-small largest roots
